coredata.dc:title: Green synthesized carbon quantum dots from Prosopis juliflora leaves as a dual off-on fluorescence probe for sensing mercury (II) and chemet drug
coredata.dc:creator: Pourreza, Nahid
Ghomi, Matineh
coredata.dc:description: In this article, a facile and green dual fluorescence sensor was developed for mercury and chemet as an anti-poisoning drug for heavy metal ions. The method is based on “off-on” fluorescence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared from Prosopis juliflora leaves as a green and low cost source. The synthesis of CQDs was easily performed with a rapid one pot process and the prepared CQDs exhibited a blue luminescence with a 5% quantum yield. The off fluorescent is observed in the presence of different concentrations of mercury (II) that is specifically recovered with the addition of chemet. The gradually recovered fluorescence is due to the high affinity of chelating property of succimer drug towards mercury (II) with a good restoration. Under the optimized condition, two linear calibrations were validated in the range of 5–500 ng mL−1 and 2.5–22.5 ng mL−1 for Hg (II) and chemet, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) based on 3Sb was 1.26 ng mL−1 (or 6.3 nM) for Hg (II) and 1.4 ng mL−1 for chemet. The relative standard deviation of the fluorescent probe for 50 ng mL−1 of Hg (II) and 10 ng mL−1 of chemet was 4.5% and 1.3% (n = 10), respectively. This methodology was applied to successful measurements of Hg (II) and chemet in the water and human serum samples, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Hg (II)
Chemet
Off-on fluorescence
Prosopis juliflora
Water samples
Human serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and optical properties of CQDs
Effect of pH changing on the probe performance
Application of CQDs probe in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) Typical TEM images of synthesize CQDs at different magnification histogram of particle diameter distribution of CQDs (inset). Characterizations of CQDs: (c) FTIR spectrum in the frequency range of 400–4000 cm−1 and (d) XRD pattern for CDQs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and the emission spectra of synthesized CQDs excited at different wavelengths (310–380 nm). Inset: the photograph of a green CQDs solution under white (right) and UV-light (left) at λex = 350 nm and (b) the emission spectra of CQDs, CQDs in the presence of Hg (II) and Hg (II)-chemet. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The effect of different pH on the CQDs probe at two excitation wavelength of 325 nm and 350 nm and (b) the effect of adding various concentrations of two salts on fluorescence intensity of CQDs probe.
Effect of time on the restoration of CQDs fluorescence after the addition of chemet.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs probe upon addition of different concentration of Hg (II) ion (0 to 500 ng mL−1) at optimal conditions and the corresponding linear calibration curve for off-state of probe (b) fluorescence spectra of CQDs probe upon addition of different concentration of chemet (0 to 12.5 ng mL−1) at fix concentration of Hg (II) (250 ng mL−1) under optimal conditions and the corresponding linear calibration curve for on-state of probe.

(a) The influence of some potential interfering species on the corresponding intensity of CQDs at off state (blue column), the mixture of CQDs-Hg (II) (200 ng mL−1) designated as A, the mixture of CQDs-Hg (II) and different species (1000 fold), Pd2+ (10-fold) and Se(IV) (500-fold), CQDs-Hg (II)-Fe3+ and CQDs-Hg (II)-Cu2+ at the same concentration. (b) The influence of some potential interfering species on the corresponding intensity of CQDs at on state (red column), the mixture of CQDs-Hg (II) (200 ng mL−1) in the presence of chemet (20 ng mL−1), CQDs-Fe3+ and CQDs-Cu2+. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs alone and in the presence of high concentration of Hg (II) and other metal ions (10 fold of Hg (II) concentrations).
Schematic illustration of off-on fluorescence probe based on synthesis of new green CQDs.
Determination of QY of carbon dots at different percentage of dispersion media with quinine sulphate as a reference.
Comparison of the proposed method with some of different methods for Hg (II) determination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results for Hg (II) determination (n = 3) in different water samples by the developed method at optimal conditions.

row.entry: Analytical results for chemet determination in serum sample by developed method

head.ce:title: Green synthesized carbon quantum dots from Prosopis juliflora leaves as a dual off-on fluorescence probe for sensing mercury (II) and chemet drug

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz, Ahvaz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, a facile and green dual fluorescence sensor was developed for mercury and chemet as an anti-poisoning drug for heavy metal ions. The method is based on “off-on” fluorescence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared from Prosopis juliflora leaves as a green and low cost source. The synthesis of CQDs was easily performed with a rapid one pot process and the prepared CQDs exhibited a blue luminescence with a 5% quantum yield. The off fluorescent is observed in the presence of different concentrations of mercury (II) that is specifically recovered with the addition of chemet. The gradually recovered fluorescence is due to the high affinity of chelating property of succimer drug towards mercury (II) with a good restoration. Under the optimized condition, two linear calibrations were validated in the range of 5–500 ng mL−1 and 2.5–22.5 ng mL−1 for Hg (II) and chemet, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) based on 3Sb was 1.26 ng mL−1 (or 6.3 nM) for Hg (II) and 1.4 ng mL−1 for chemet. The relative standard deviation of the fluorescent probe for 50 ng mL−1 of Hg (II) and 10 ng mL−1 of chemet was 4.5% and 1.3% (n = 10), respectively. This methodology was applied to successful measurements of Hg (II) and chemet in the water and human serum samples, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the very important task in environmental analysis is the determination of heavy metals ions in water samples for human healthy life protection. Despite the numerous research reported, there is still a challenge in the field of trace metal ion determination with high selectivity and sensitivity. Significantly, one of the most popular environmental pollutants is the mercury ion which can accumulate in the human body and lead to irreversible damages to the human nerve system and can result chronic diseases. Thus the mercury determination using rapid, simple and highly sensitive methodology in aqueous solution is very notable in environmental monitoring and clinical toxicology [1–5]. Lately, extensive research has been applied for Hg (II) detection such as electrochemical method [6], mass spectrometry [7], colorimetric method [8], HPLC coupled atomic fluorescence spectrometry [9], atomic fluorescence spectrometry [10] and atomic force microscopy [11]. Nevertheless, the shortcoming of these techniques such as time consuming, complicated method and expensive equipment can limit the routine determination of Hg (II).

Different probes including nanoparticles based sensors [12], fluorescent sensor [13], chemosensor [14], microfluidic sensor [15], optode sensor [16] and colorimetric sensor [17] have also been developed for Hg (II) detection. Great efforts have been devoted to explore a sensing method based on fluorescence spectroscopy as an ideal optical technique for Hg (II) determination. The sensing based on fluorescence technique have many advantages such as non-destructive for materials, ultra-sensitive detection, fast and simple responses [18].

Among some chelating agents used for treating Hg (II) exposure, meso‑2,3‑dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA-Succimer, Chemet) is an ideal candidate. Chemet is an orally active, chelating agent used for treatment of heavy metal poisoning such as mercury [19]. According to the chemet structure, the existence of two S-atom placed in juxta position can act as a binding sites for metal ions. This chelating agent does not show any fluorescence emission. Moreover, it has been found that the available chelators have different efficiency, administration way and side effects. Chemet was the most efficient chelator which has been approved by the guidance of United States Food and Drug Administration (US-FDA) [20].

The Prosopis Juliflora is a shrub or small tree and a member of the guminosae family, the genus of Prosopis includes 44 species which are classified in 5 sections and 6 series [21]. During the past 150 years the species of Prosopis have been reported for many applications such as using the wood as a fuel and fodder for livestock in various countries. It is notable that this species can grow in the poorest soils where no plants can grow due to the high environment tolerance at high temperature and low annual rainfall [21,22].

Recently, fluorescent nanoparticles have attracted high attention in extending fluorescent probes owing to their unique optical characteristic. The most usual carbon based nanomaterial as a fluorescent nanoparticle is CQD [23–27]. The carbon dots and quantum dots are expected to be replacing the conventional phosphor materials in recent future because of their excellent optical, luminescent, physical and chemical properties [27–31]. Also, the applications of traditional metallic quantum dots such as CdS, CdSe and PbSe to fabricate the fluorescent probes are limited due to some problems such as toxicity, hydrophobicity, high cost and complicated procedures of synthesis [32,33]. Hence, the CQD as a new type of nanocarbon family has been the subject of considerable researches [34–36].

Due to the advantages of fluorescent CQDs such as the facile synthetic routes, low photo-bleaching, low cytotoxicity, fluorescence non-blinking emission, water-solubility and easy functionalization have made them a good source for new fluorescent sensor other than organic dyes and metallic quantum dots [37–40]. CQDs can be prepared from different sources such as citric acid, sucrose, glucose, etc. Some of these preparations require long operations [41]. Therefore, green, easy and straight forward methods for the preparation of CQDs are still a challenge. Many facile protocols have been applied for the synthesis of CQDs such as ultrasonic passivation technique [30], hydrothermal synthesis [42] and direct laser ablation [43].

Various kinds of natural carbon sources [44–47] can be used to produce CQDs due to their availability and non-toxicity. The present work includes a simple and fast hydrothermal method for green generation of CQDs from Prosopis juliflora leaves and its application as a switching off-on fluorescence probe for Hg (II) ion and chemet drug.

Chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Milli-Q water was used in all the experiments. Standard stock solution of Hg (II) (1000 mg mL−1) was purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). More diluted solutions were prepared by successive dilution of this stock solution. Chemet drug was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Britton–Robinson buffer solutions were prepared by the addition of suitable proportion volumes of sodium hydroxide 0.2 mol L−1 to a mixture of H3BO3, H3PO4 and CH3COOH (0.04 mol L−1 each) and adjusting to the desired pH using a pH meter. Ammonia buffer pH 8 was prepared by gradual addition of HCl (0.1 mol L−1) to the ammonia solution (0.05 mol L−1). Also phosphate buffer pH 8 was prepared by the addition of sodium hydroxide (1 mol L−1) to phosphoric acid solution (0.1 mol L−1). A stock solution of NaCl (0.2 mol L−1) was prepared.

UV–Visible absorption spectra were obtained using a Varian Cary 100 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (California, USA).The fluorescence measurements were carried using a Shimadzu Model SRF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan) equipped with Xenon lamp as an excitation source. All fluorescence spectra of CQDs were measured using a 3 mL standard quartz cuvette and both excitation and emission band widths were set at 5 nm and taken at room temperature. FTIR characterization was investigated to verify the functional groups by a Bomem 102 FT-IR spectrometer (Canada) using KBr pellets as the sample matrix in the frequency region of 400–4000 cm−1.

The crystallinity of carbon dots was determined using a powder X-ray diffraction methodology. The X-ray diffraction instrument (Philips analytical X-ray B.V. Netherlands) was set by Cu anode tube as follow. The generator of current and tension were 30 mA and 40 kV with step size (2θ) 0.020, respectively. The diffraction intensity of Cu-K ∝ radiation (λ = 0.1541 nm) was investigated between start angle of 10.010 and end angle of 89.990.
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Nano ZS (red badge) ZEN 3600), Malvern Company (England) was recorded to investigate the size of CQDs.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images were recorded using a Zeiss-EM10C transmission electron microscope (Germany) operating at 200 kV. pH was adjusted using a digital Metrohm pH-Meter model 632 (Herisau, Switzerland).

The typical experimental preparation of CQD is described as follows: 1 g of Prosopis juliflora leaves were washed with water. Subsequently, the leaves were put into a crucible, heated at 200 °C for about 30 min followed by grinding to powder and heating at 200 °C for about 1 h. The black carbonized powder of leaves was cooled to room temperature. 100 mg of carbonized black powder was dispersed in 10 mL of acetic acid (50% V/V) and also 100 mg was dispersed in 10 mL of ethanol as a dispersion media and kept for 24 h. Then the mixture was centrifuged to remove agglomerate and precipitated particles and the light brown supernatant liquid including CQDs was collected and preserved for further research. The prepared CQDs were also freeze-dried for calculation of Quantum yield (QY).

Quantum yield determination was carried out in accordance with the previously certified method [48,49]. The fluorescence intensity of quinine sulphate solution (in 0.1 mol L−1 of H2SO4) was selected as a standard (literature QY is 54% at 360 nm) as described before. The base of QY calculation is comparative method [50]. By keeping the absorbance of both the suspension of CQDs and standard sample under 0.10 at 350 nm, the absorption effects were minimized and the QY was calculated by the following equation:
ϕ c
=
ϕ q
A c
A q
I c
/
I q
η q
/
η c
where the фc and фq refer to fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs and quinine sulphate, Ac and Aq indicate the absorbance value of CQD and quinine sulphate at the excitation of 350 nm and η is the refractive index of the solvent which was 1.33. The QY of CQDs was found to be about 5% by freeze-dried method.

For detection of Hg (II) based on designed off-on green fluorescent probe, 1 mL of the CQDs (1 mg mL−1), 0.5 mL of ammonia buffer (pH 8) and 0.1 mL of sodium chloride (0.2 mol L−1) was transferred to a 2 volumetric flask followed by the addition of different concentration of Hg (II) (final concentration in the range of 5–500 ng mL−1). The volumetric flask was made up to the mark with water. The associated quenching intensities (F) were recorded at 437 nm after excitation at 350 nm. Same condition without Hg (II) adding was used as a blank and the fluorescence intensity was recorded (F0).

For determination of chemet, various concentrations of chemet (final concentration in the range of 2.5–22.5 ng mL−1) were added to the designed CQDs probe with defined concentration of Hg (II) (250 ng mL−1). In order to complete the reaction, the suspension solutions were remained static for 2 min at room temperature and the restoration intensity of the probe was measured under excitation at 350 nm. The blank was a solution of CQDs at optimal condition with defined Hg (II) concentration. The fluorescence intensity was recorded for the blank (F0) and in the presence of chemet (F). Schematic illustration of the off-on the probe is shown in Scheme 1 .

The water samples were filtered by filter paper to remove particulate matters and were used for sensing of Hg (II). To validate the reliability of proposed new sensing system for chemet determination, fresh human serum was tested. To precipitate proteins of plasma, 1.5 mL trichloroacetic acid was added to 1 mL of plasma solution [51], the mixture was vortex, centrifuge and filtered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized in two dispersion media ethanol and acetic acid (50% V/V). The acetic acid media illustrated more intense blue fluorescence under UV lamp and we also investigated the effect of percentage of dispersion media on QY as shown in Table 1 . It was noted that, when the percentages of acetic acid are decreased, the CQDs formation is also decreased. Hence this polar solvent was selected for further investigation.

The microscopic morphologies of as-prepared CQDs were characterized by TEM image. The TEM images show that CQDs are homogeneously distributed in the solution with uniform size (Fig. 1 a, b). The average size of distribution histogram of mono-dispersed CQDs illustrates the spherical shapes with an average size of 5.8 nm (the inset of Fig. 1b).

For better identification of the structure and functional groups of CQDs, the FT-IR spectrum was obtained (Fig. 1c). The peaks emerged at 456 cm−1 and 530 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibrations of CC and alkyl halides, respectively. The peak at 803 cm−1 originated from CH stretching vibrations. Two absorption bands of stretching vibrations at 1414 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 are assigned to CC with aromatic ring stretching and amide banding. Moreover, two peak at 1716 cm−1 and 2980 cm−1 for CO and OH stretching vibration bands verify the existence of carboxylic acid or the functional groups containing oxygen. Also, the peaks at 1025 cm−1 and 3448 cm−1 correspond to the CO and NH/OH stretching vibrations, respectively which is an indication of the presence of amino-containing functional groups and amide functional groups. In order to obtain structural information of CQDs, the X-ray diffractogram was recorded (Fig. 1d). The spectrum of diffraction peaks at four diffraction angles of 2Ɵ = 11.54 and 2Ɵ = 22.12, 2Ɵ = 22.29 and 2Ɵ = 42.42 is observed. The most intense peaks at diffraction angles of 2θ = 11.54 and 2θ = 22.12 are usually observed for CQDs. The presence of intense peak at 2Ɵ = 22.12 and weak peak at 2Ɵ = 42.42 are related to the plane of (533) and (101) diffraction pattern of plane of graphitic carbon [52].

Since the emission peaks are dependent on the excitation wavelength, different excitation spectra were taken for synthesized CQDs. Two excitation wavelengths of 325 nm and 350 nm were selected. Two emission peaks of 396 nm and 437 nm were observed at fixed excitation wavelength of 325 nm and 350 nm, respectively which reveals that two kinds of transitions are occurred.

To further study the optical properties of the green CQDs probe, the UV–Vis spectrum properties were investigated. The UV–Vis absorption peak was taken as shown in Fig. 2a. This spectrum clearly illustrates a broad peak. This broad peak is assigned to the aromatic π-π* system or the n–p* transition [53–55]. The digital picture of the aqueous dispersions of CQDs illustrated as the inset in Fig. 2a shows that the green CQDs are water soluble and emit very strong bright blue luminescence under UV radiation. Since chlorophylls and Mg porphyrins compounds widely exist in the leaves of any plants, the previously reported UV–Vis spectra of chlorophylls a and b [56] and the spectrum of the synthesized CQDs were compared with each other. It is observed that the characteristic absorption peak of chlorophyll does not exist in the synthesized CQDs. In acidic media, magnesium ions are released from chlorophylls and the brown pigments of pheophytin A and pheophytin B are formed. The UV–Vis spectra of pheophytins are at 666 nm and 434 nm, respectively [57]. Regarding to the black color of carbonized powder of leaves and process of CQDs in acidic media, it seems that chlorophyll does not exist in the prepared CQDs. Moreover, luminescence and absorption spectra of porphyrins and metalloporphyrins [58] show no similar spectra like this reported CQD. Hence the CQDs cannot contain the materials stated above.

The different excitation spectra taken for synthesized CQDs at different excitation spectra shown in Fig. 2a clearly indicate that more intense peak is observed at 437 nm. The figure also shows the excitation dependent of CQDs, which is attributed to the quantum effect and different emission traps on the CQDs surface.

The original fluorescence intensity of CQDs solution was gradually quenched by the addition of different concentration of Hg (II). It is believed that Hg (II) could be coordinated by N-containing functional groups [59–62] on the surface and CO, COOH groups of the edge of CQDs. The approaching process of CQDs in the presence of Hg (II) induce the electrostatic interaction, non-radiative recombination of excitations through electron transfer between oxygen containing group of CQD and Hg (II) leading to the fluorescence quenching [63,64].

In the second step, upon the addition of different concentrations of chemet, Hg (II) is strongly captured and the surface of carbon dots gets free of Hg (II) ion. The mercury cation has a particular binding preference to chemet [65] because of its specific steric structure and very good coordination sites (sulphur and carboxyl group). As a consequence, the releasing process can easily happen for Hg (II) attached to CQDs due to decomposing of CQDs-link-Hg. As depicted in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs is recovered and switched to the “on” state after treatment of the Hg-CQDs solution with chemet solution. A very small change can be seen in the shoulder of fluorescence spectra at “on” state which could probably be due to the presence of chemet in the media and its interaction with Hg (II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH changing on the probe performance
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical medium (acidic or basic) could have an effect on the fluorescence behaviour of CQD. Thus the relationship between different pH and emission intensity at two excitation wavelengths (325 nm and 350 nm) was carefully studied. As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence intensity differences (F0 − F) at excitation wavelength of 350 nm show better results. Therefore, the excitation wavelength of 350 nm was selected for further research. In more basic or acidic media, the emission spectra illustrated a blue shift and lower intensity was obtained. In acidic media, the lower intensity may be due to inducing proton charge on the functional groups which inhibits or disrupts the electronic transition of the surface defects. In high basic media, the fluorescence quenching could happen due to the destruction of the surface. Also no aggregation was observed in the pH range of 6–9. In order to achieve a good detection performance; different buffer medium such as ammonia, phosphate and Britton -Robinson buffers were tested. Ammonia buffer in comparison with two other buffers shows better result. Thus ammonia buffer (pH 8) was selected as optimal medium for further research.

This effect was checked using two kinds of salts. Different concentrations of NaCl and KCl were added to a 2 mL volumetric flask under optimum condition stated above. The results show that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was decreased upon increasing of each salt due to the photo-instability of CQDs at high concentrations of each electrolyte [44]. But at the lower salt concentration, the electrostatic repulsion of NaCl showed a positive effect on the quenching process by bringing Hg (II) ions close to the CQDs surface. The main reason of this phenomenon may be related to the surrounding factors of the surface state or molecular state which affected the emission spectra [66]. Thus the NaCl concentration (0.01 M) was selected for further research at optimal condition (Fig. 3b).

The effect of reaction time was investigated at two situations, First, the off-state procedure and second, the on-state procedure. For the off-state, no fluorescence changing was recorded at different times at optimal condition and the fluorescence response occurred immediately after the addition of Hg (II). The on-state process was investigated after the addition of 12.5 ng mL−1 of chemet to the probe containing 200 ng mL−1 of Hg (II) using different time intervals at room temperature. According to the obtained results (Fig. 4 ) a reaction time of 2 min was selected as the real-time monitoring for restoration of fluorescence at optimized conditions.

Two parameters such as dynamic range and LOD are very important factor in the design of any sensor assay. The Stern–Volmer equation (F0/F − 1 = KSVC) was used to process the fluorescence quenching [67], F and F0 were defined earlier and Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant. Under the optimum condition, the developed probe was linear in the range of 5–500 ng mL−1 of Hg (II) as illustrated in Fig. 5a. The linear regression equation is F/F0 = −0.0012 CHg (II) + 0.9993 (r = 0.9965). The limit of detection (LOD) based on 3Sb was 1.26 ng mL−1 (or 6.3 nM) for Hg (II) and limit of quantification (10 Sb) was 4.20 ng mL−1. The relative standard deviation was 4.5 and 1.7% for two concentrations of 50 ng mL−1 and 350 ng mL−1, respectively.

To study the linear relationship between the amount of chemet and F/F0, different concentrations of chemet were added to the solution at the optimum concentration of Hg (II) (200 ng mL−1). The reason for choosing this concentration of Hg (II) can be expressed as follow. At lower concentrations of Hg (II), the fluorescence intensity cannot be quenched completely and no clear restoration was seen. Beside, at excess amount of Hg (II), the complex of free Hg (II) with chemet was formed instead of creating complex of CQDs with Hg (II), which resulted in low sensitivity. The calibration was linear in the range of 2.5–22.5 ng mL−1 for chemet with the equation of F/F0 = 0.0194Cchemet + 1.0276 (r = 0.9952). The limit of detection (LOD) based on 3Sb was 1.4 ng mL−1 and limit of quantification (10 Sb) was 4.7 ng mL−1 for chemet. The relative standard deviation was 1.3% and 3.9% (n = 10) for 10 and 20 ng mL−1 of chemet, respectively. The on-state spectra are illustrated in Fig. 5b. For comparison, Table 2 presented the characteristics of some of the proposed sensor for Hg (II) detection. As depicted in the Table 2 the LOD and linear range of this sensor is better or comparable to some of the previously reported sensors.

To explore the selectivity of green CQDs for the determination of Hg (II), the off-on state of CQDs was investigated under the optimal conditions. In these experiments, solutions containing constant concentration of Hg (II) and 1000-fold excess concentrations of other species were treated under the general procedure. A relative error higher than ±5% in the signal was regarded as interference. The obtained results indicated that Hg (II) decreased the fluorescence intensity of CDQs (designated as A in Fig. 6a) while the presence of ions such as Li+, Ba2+, Ag+, SO4 2−, Al3+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, NO3 −, NH4 +, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, PO4 3−, Br− and Ni2+ at 1000-fold excess concentration of Hg (II) ions and Pd2+ (10-fold) and Se (IV) (500-fold) under the optimal conditions did not changed the intensity of Hg-CQDs. At the off state, Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions interfered at the same concentration as Hg (II) but negligible changes were noticed at the enhancement process by the addition of a certain value of chemet. The results of this study are given in Fig. 6b.

The UV–Vis absorption of CQDs was also recorded alone and in the presence of some interfering ions. The absorption spectrums were almost unchanged after adding each metal ion (10 fold of Hg (II) concentrations) while the absorption peak of CQDs became weaker and disappeared at high concentration of Hg (II). According to these results (Fig. 7 ) and the fluorescence spectra discussed in the manuscript, the proposed sensor has high selectivity for Hg (II) detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs probe in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate of the validity and applicability of the sensing system, different real water samples were collected from Dez, Darkhoein, Bandemizan, Karoon River and Saponification Company, respectively. The developed method was used to determine Hg (II) concentration in water samples after preparation according to Section 2.6. Spiked samples were used to perform recovery tests and to examine the reliability the method. Different amount of Hg (II) was added to the samples and Hg (II) concentration was determined in each sample at optimal conditions. After evaluating the Hg (II) content of different samples the recoveries were calculated. Despite the presence of numerous organic and mineral materials existed in the water samples, the results showed that this probe provided a good validity for Hg (II) containing samples. The obtained data resulting from the average of five replicated responses are summarized in Table 3 .

For further evaluation of the accuracy of the method in real samples actually containing Hg (II), two wastewater samples from a petrochemical company (Iran) was analysed and the results were compared to those obtained by cold vapour atomic absorption spectrometry. The results presented in Table 4 indicate that there is a good agreement between the two methods. Therefore, the designed off-on fluorescence probe is capable of detecting Hg (II) in different environmental water samples with satisfactory recoveries.

The method was also used for chemet determination in plasma samples (after pre-treatment as Section 2.6). Known amounts of chemet were spiked to the serum sample for the recovery tests. The analytical results are summarized in Table 3. The results demonstrated that the proposed sensor is suitable for chemet detection in biological samples.

In this work, a novel off-on florescent sensing method was developed for selective detection of Hg (II) over many other interfering species using a green CQDs probe. The CQDs probe was prepared from Prosopis juliflora leaves as a new green source by a simple method. This probe need no expensive materials and can also be used as a sensitive, selective, label free and reproducible off-on sensing assay. The probe response is immediately quenched in the presence of different concentration of Hg (II) by static mode. The limit of detection for Hg (II) was 1.26 ng mL−1, under the optimum conditions. The turn on state occurs gradually after the addition of different concentration of chemet in aqueous media. The restoration process is completed within 2 min for chemet monitoring with high selectivity. The proposed system can be applied to the Hg (II) and chemet monitoring of the patients who have been poisoned by mercury and treated with chemet as an anti-poisoning agent. It is hoped that this sensing strategy can offer a new interest for developing green, inexpensive and sensitive sensors in environmental and biological analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz Research Council for the financial support of this work (grant 1396). The financial support of the Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council is also appreciated. The authors are sincerely grateful to Environment Protection Agency (EPA) of Khuzestan Province (Iran) for kindly providing research facilities for this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tools and tactics for the optical detection of mercuric ion
Chem. Rev.
